theorems_tried.details: |b_1| = 0 < |a_1| + 1 = 4. Śleszyński-Pringsheim does not apply.
b_n ≠ 1 — parabola theorem applies to K(a_n/1) form.
b(n) = -4n + 4 grows linearly. Σ|b_n| diverges, so Stern-Stolz divergence theorem does NOT apply. Convergence is consistent with Stern-Stolz.

convergence.proof_detail: b(n) = -4n + 4 grows linearly. Σ|b_n| diverges, so Stern-Stolz divergence theorem does NOT apply. Convergence is consistent with Stern-Stolz.

theorems_tried.details: Not a linear-b, constant-a CF. Gauss-Euler form requires specific structure.

For a(n)=2*n!, b(n)=3: The tail from n=2 onward satisfies |a(n)| = 2*n! >= |b(n)| + 1 = 4 (Sleszynski-Pringsheim on the tail). Numeric verification: depth-500 vs depth-200 error = 2.00e-100. Convergence is super-exponential (factorial decay of partial quotients).

convergence.proof_detail: For a(n)=2*n!, b(n)=3: The tail from n=2 onward satisfies |a(n)| = 2*n! >= |b(n)| + 1 = 4 (Sleszynski-Pringsheim on the tail). Numeric verification: depth-500 vs depth-200 error = 2.00e-100. Convergence is super-exponential (factorial decay of partial quotients).

convergence_check.proof_sketch: For a(n) = k·n!, b(n) = B (constant): the n-th tail T_n of the CF satisfies T_n = k·n!/(B + T_{n+1}). Since |T_{n+1}| grows as (n+1)!, we get |T_n| ~ k·n!/((n+1)!) = k/(n+1) → 0. The tails converge super-exponentially fast, guaranteeing convergence of the full CF.
flags: Numerical convergence: depth-200 vs depth-100 error = 2.18e-60

proof_targets.proof_strategy: 1. Establish convergence via named CF theorem (Worpitzky, Śleszyński-Pringsheim, Van Vleck, or ratio test). 2. If polynomial coefficients: express as a ratio of contiguous    hypergeometric functions and apply Gauss/Euler CF theorems. 3. Attempt CAS identification of limit via Bessel/₁F₁ ratios +    SymPy nsimplify. 4. Verify symbolic closed form via high-precision evaluation.
proof_targets.framework: Generalised continued fraction theory (Wall 1948, Lorentzen & Waadeland 2008)
key_lemmas: Convergence: apply named theorem to CF with a(n)=[], b(n)=[]
Closed form: Bessel ratio I_ν(z)/I_{ν-1}(z) or confluent ₁F₁ ratio
Symbolic verification: CAS check to 100+ digits
Uniqueness: rule out algebraic/PSLQ relations to known constants
